text:
A decision has been made on the future of the Australian swimming coach who openly expressed his support to a competitor from another country instead of his own Dolphins team.

An Australian swimming coach has been spared from being thrown out of the Paris Olympics after he publicly expressed support to a rival athlete instead of his own squad.

Michael Palfrey reportedly told South Korean media on Tuesday that he hoped world champion Kim Woo-Min would win the 400 metre freestyle this weekend, despite two Aussies Sam Short and Elijah Winnington also set to compete in the event.

“I really hope he can win, but ultimately I really hope he swims well," Palfrey, who also coached Kim, said, before he declared "Go Korea" at the end of the interview.

Palfrey was confronted by Australian officials, including Dolphins head coach Rohan Taylor, who described the actions as "un-Australian".

"I need to look at the performance of the team and make a judgement call. He gave us the information that he remembered saying it, so the general consensus from Michael was that he did say things promoting the other athlete," Taylor said.

"We confronted him with those details and he owns that and he was taken to task on it and he is very remorseful… the guidelines were pretty clear.

“It’s very disappointing to me that a coach of ours is promoting another athlete.”

Taylor confirmed on Friday morning Palfrey will remain in the squad for the next nine days, saying it was "the best decision" following consultations with the Australian Olympic Committee, other Dolphins coaches and athletes.

"We just had a team meeting, online team meet, where everyone was on there where Michael apologised profusely for his mistake", he told Channel 9.

"Again... I'm very critical of him. I believe it was a very bad error of judgment. And the consequences for that will be coming in the future when we get back home."

Palfrey is one of eight Australian coaches.

While talking about the 400m freestyle event in the South Korean interview earlier this week, he described the race will likely be a "dogfight" and "rough".

"There will be three or four of them in the race that think they can win it," he said

“I think it’s going to come down to that last 100 metres. We need Woo-min to lift, that’s what I’ll be saying to him. He’s got to race tough.”

Palfrey was wearing Australian team colours when he made the remarks.

He had months earlier been told to disassociate with overseas swimmers.

Asked about Palfrey's comments, Winnington said: "I couldn't care less."

The 24-year-old Queenslander revealed the coach had apologised.

"I don’t think he meant what he said. He’s a great guy and great coach," he told Nine.

“Obviously Woo-min trains with him but he’s come up to me and knows he didn’t mean what he said. At the end of the day, it’s just a comment and I’m here to race the best I can. There’s no animosity among the team. It’s all good."

Winnington won silver, while Kim took out the gold at the World Aquatics Championships in Germany earlier this year.

title: Australian swimming coach allowed to stay with Dolphins team after expressing support to South Korean rival in Paris Olympics | Sky News Australia
description: An Australian swimming coach has been spared from being thrown out of the Paris Olympics after he publicly expressed support to a rival athlete instead of his own squad.
